title: ‘The Banshees of Inisherin’ and Top 5 Movie Friendships
format: Sean Fennessey, Amanda Dobbins, and Chris Ryan review Martin McDonagh's 'The Banshees of Inisherin' and handicap the Best Actor race, then rank the best movie friendships on film, with a few extra picks from Sean along the way. Sean also talks to McDonagh about the Irish Civil War backdrop, his visual influences, and reuniting with Brendan Gleeson and Colin Farrell.

segments.heading: ‘The Banshees of Inisherin’
segments.note: Sean Fennessey, Amanda Dobbins, Chris Ryan

films.title: The Banshees of Inisherin
films.note: Martin McDonagh's fourth feature, reuniting Brendan Gleeson and Colin Farrell from In Bruges: on a remote island off 1920s Ireland, fiddle player Colm (Gleeson) abruptly ends his friendship with Pádraic (Farrell), then escalates to cutting off his own fingers to enforce the break. Chris calls it his favorite of the year outside Top Gun: Maverick, praising the swing from Coen-brothers-esque romp to devastation. Amanda reads it as McDonagh splitting himself into two halves, art-and-legacy versus pub-and-pleasantness, and ties the 1920s Irish Civil War backdrop to the question of how much you owe yourself versus other people. Both single out Kerry Condon as Siobhán and Barry Keoghan as Dominic, debate whether Farrell's performance is awards-bound given how bleak the film gets, and admire the hand-knit sweaters and Vermeer-style production design.

films.title: In Bruges
films.note: McDonagh and Gleeson/Farrell's 2008 breakout, barely seen in America on release per McDonagh and reassessed into cult status only after Farrell's Golden Globe and McDonagh's screenplay nomination. Sean rewatched it the night before but couldn't finish (the dead-kid material is 'tough'), and both hosts note the tonal mirror-flip between Gleeson's and Farrell's roles from that film to Banshees.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: Called simultaneously McDonagh's 'most complex' and, per Amanda, 'probably his worst' film. It's the only one of his with a female protagonist, and it's drawn controversy over how it handles anger and redemption.

films.title: Seven Psychopaths
films.note: Brief mention for its dog acting; McDonagh calls it a tonal 'blip' in the interview, with less of the internal sadness that runs through his other films.

films.title: Six Shooter
films.note: McDonagh's Oscar-winning short and his first collaboration with Brendan Gleeson, years before In Bruges.

films.title: Elvis
films.note: Austin Butler's performance is 'pretty incredible' per both hosts, even though Chris didn't like the film; Amanda enjoyed watching it despite being 'deaf' to Elvis as a cultural figure.

films.title: Living
films.note: Bill Nighy's late-career turn, an English-language remake of Kurosawa's Ikiru from Sony Pictures Classics. Neither host has seen it yet.

films.title: The Son
films.note: Hugh Jackman floated as a contender; neither host has seen it.

films.title: Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
films.note: Daniel Giménez Cacho mentioned as a possible nominee.

films.title: Bones and All
films.note: Timothée Chalamet mentioned as a maybe.

films.title: Babylon
films.note: Brad Pitt's supporting-vs-lead Oscar-category confusion comes up, along with Diego Calva's actual lead-actor status. Neither host has seen it; they clock the runtime at three hours eight minutes.

films.title: The Whale
films.note: Brendan Fraser, described as the biggest obstacle to a Farrell win. Chris saw it that week and describes a standing ovation and an emotional post-screening Q&A.

films.title: Inside Out
films.note: Prompted by Chris's plug for a Disney+ shorts recap show. Sean didn't expect to rewatch it but calls it masterful, and gets asked point-blank whether he cried at Bing Bong's death.

segments.heading: Top Five Movie Friendships
segments.note: Chris and Amanda's ranked fives, plus a few extras Sean threw in

picks.title: Midnight Run
picks.note: No. 1. Charles Grodin's Jonathan Mardukas and Robert De Niro's Jack Walsh aren't friends for 99% of the film, then comes the LAX-terminal kindness Chris calls the best depiction of friendship in movie history. He and Sean compare it to their own decade-plus working relationship.

picks.title: Ocean's Eleven
picks.note: No. 2. George Clooney's Danny Ocean and Brad Pitt's Rusty Ryan have a wordless rapport, finishing each other's sentences. Chris texted ahead to check it was okay to use it; Amanda skipped it because she talks about the Ocean's movies too much already.

picks.title: My Own Private Idaho
picks.note: No. 3. River Phoenix and Keanu Reeves, loosely built on Henry IV. Chris points to the flophouse Shakespeare scene as proof 'these guys just love hanging out'; River Phoenix was a formative, slightly older figure for him growing up.

picks.title: The Shawshank Redemption
picks.note: No. 4. Tim Robbins and Morgan Freeman as Andy and Red, a friendship rendered over decades from wariness to bond. Chris pegs himself as a Red; Sean calls him an Andy.

picks.title: The Sting
picks.note: No. 5. Robert Redford's Johnny Hooker and Paul Newman's Henry Gondorff, con men who 'got on like a house on fire.' Chris nearly picked Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid instead.

picks.title: Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid
picks.note: Sidecar to No. 5. Chris finds it mythic, if 'expired pop'; Amanda finds it dull, 'a lot of riding bikes in circles.'

picks.title: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
picks.note: No. 1. Elliott and E.T. play out the idealized childhood-friend fantasy: someone who fills the voids left by imperfect parents and neighborhood kids. Amanda wept for twenty minutes rewatching it on The Rewatchables while walking her infant son around the neighborhood.

picks.title: Stand by Me
picks.note: Sidecar to No. 1. One of the childhood-friendship alternates Amanda weighed against E.T., alongside My Own Private Idaho.

picks.title: Good Will Hunting
picks.note: No. 2. Matt Damon and Ben Affleck, the 'best part of my day' speech. Amanda identifies as a closed-off Chuckie type rather than a Will.

picks.title: Lost in Translation
picks.note: No. 3. Bill Murray's Bob and Scarlett Johansson's Charlotte have a bond that doesn't exist until the film creates it, one Amanda reads as at least partly a romance. A non-negotiable Sofia Coppola pick.

picks.title: Ferris Bueller's Day Off
picks.note: No. 4. Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck as Ferris and Cameron. Amanda argues Ferris being a bad-for-you friend is true to how teenage friendship actually works; one fan theory floated is that Ferris is a figment of Cameron's imagination.

picks.title: The First Wives Club
picks.note: No. 5. Bette Midler, Goldie Hawn, and Diane Keaton team up on their exes and close the film singing 'You Don't Own Me' in white suits, backing up Amanda's thesis that female-friendship movies of the '80s through the 2000s peak with a choreographed number.

picks.title: Husbands
picks.note: Ben Gazzara, Peter Falk, and John Cassavetes as three friends processing a fourth's death. 'Not necessarily healthy friendships,' Sean says, 'but I think it's important to show those too.'

picks.title: Toy Story
picks.note: Woody and Buzz, prompted by watching his daughter light up at Toy Story 2 in a waiting room, an archetypal friendship he traces back to Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid.

picks.title: California Split
picks.note: George Segal and Elliott Gould as gambling buddies who are 'really bad for each other.' Sean's pick for his favorite movie ever made, on any given day.

picks.title: Mississippi Grind
picks.note: Sidecar to the California Split pick. A 'full-blown remake' per Sean, and one of Ryan Reynolds's best performances, with Ben Mendelsohn 'at his peak.'

picks.title: 25th Hour
picks.note: Edward Norton, Philip Seymour Hoffman, and Barry Pepper as a fractured high-school trio. Sean connects it to how his own friendships from that era have or haven't held up.

films.title: Days of Heaven
films.note: McDonagh's main visual touchstone for Banshees, aiming for that landscape beauty and imagery, animals included.

films.title: Badlands
films.note: The other Malick reference point for tone and image.

films.title: The Night of the Hunter
films.note: Surfaces, per McDonagh, in the shadows of Brendan Gleeson's room.

films.title: Tár
films.note: One of two new films McDonagh caught that weekend 'checking out the competition'; he calls it, with Triangle of Sadness, evidence of an interesting fall for movies.

films.title: Triangle of Sadness
films.note: The second film from that same weekend.

films.title: Women Talking
films.note: Seen at Toronto; McDonagh calls it brilliant, the kind of film where, if it wins everything, 'it's okay because we're beaten by the right people.'

Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of T.title: BARDO, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths

Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid.title: Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid
